summaries.Lydia Chilton:
Category: Lydia Chilton  

This folder captures nearly all traffic from Prof. Lydia Chilton (and occasional third-party forwards she initiates). Messages revolve around:  
• Scheduling or rescheduling 1-on-1 and group meetings (Double Agents, DAPLab lunches, hack-a-thons).  
• Quick progress check-ins (“can you come to my office?”) or lightweight technical questions.  
• Sharing resources, internship calls, reimbursement/administrative links, and Calendly invites.  
• Forwarded opportunities or FYIs that require no immediate reply.

Sender intent is usually fast coordination or a brief status pulse; replies are expected the same business day, often within the hour when time-sensitive.  

Triage:  
1. Scan subject for dates/times. If a meeting change is proposed, update Google Calendar and confirm quickly.  
2. For progress queries, reply with current status and next steps.  
3. File shared links in the project knowledge base; no reply needed unless a question is posed.  
4. Tag administrative items (“reimbursement”, “forms”) for later tracking but acknowledge receipt.

Style: keep replies concise (≤3 sentences when possible), proactive, and upbeat; mirror Lydia’s informal yet professional tone.
summaries.Apartment:
Apartment Category – Summary (for tenants/household logistics)

Scope: Messages tied to renting and managing a residence. Includes • apartment search/lease negotiation (brokers, listing replies, application status) • lease renewal or surrender paperwork and billing notices • move-in/move-out coordination, elevator/service bookings, utility setup • ongoing property-management traffic: maintenance scheduling, vendor entry, exterminator visits, flood/repair updates, weather prep alerts • building-wide or campus public-safety bulletins relevant to the address • package-tracking or third-party service bookings (TaskRabbit, Wayfair, etc.)

Typical sender intent: secure signatures or payments by a deadline, confirm appointments, report/resolve maintenance issues, or broadcast FYIs that may require tenant preparation.

Triage & response: 1. Time-sensitive (lease offers, renewal deadlines, service elevator, vendor ETA) → same-day reply; calendar the commitment and file confirmation. 2. Maintenance/utility issues → acknowledge, provide details/photos, and track until closed. 3. Broadcast alerts (weather, crime) → no reply needed; archive after noting any action. 4. Advertising/open-house mailers → skim; delete or archive.

Style: reply promptly, reference unit/address, list preferred contact numbers, attach docs as PDF, and CC all parties when requested (“Reply All”).
summaries.Other:
Scope  
This bucket catches low-urgency, non-transactional messages: spam-filter digests (e.g., “Spam and Low Priority Email Digest”), news round-ups (Gothamist, Washington Post, FT), promotional blasts (Uber Eats, Ticketmaster, Volo Sports), and bulk academic listserv notices that don’t need immediate action.

Sender intent & common asks  
• Provide aggregated spam for optional review or release.  
• Market products, events, or subscriptions.  
• Share general news or community announcements.  
Rarely do they request a direct reply; the call-to-action is usually “click here,” “donate,” or “unsubscribe.”

Triage & response strategy  
1. Scan subject lines once daily (or weekly) for anything unexpectedly important.  
2. Delete or archive by default; no reply is expected.  
3. For recurring promos you never use, unsubscribe to cut clutter.  
4. If a digest flags a legitimate message, retrieve it from the quarantine folder, then whitelist the sender.  
5. Report anything phishing-like to IT security.

Style/policy notes  
Keep interactions minimal, avoid sharing personal data, and never forward spam internally.
summaries.SEAS:
SEAS Category Summary (Engineering Student Affairs & Community)

What’s here  
• School-wide newsletters (CU@SEAS, Dean/EGSC “Weekly”) listing events, shuttles, facilities work, socials, career panels.  
• Administrative notices from SEAS Academics, Evaluation Administrators, Admissions, CCPE, etc. Examples: orientation tutorial holds, course-evaluation reminders, degree-conferral and advanced-standing decisions, missing transcripts, stipend updates, grant calls.  

Sender intent & common asks  
• Inform or remind students about upcoming deadlines, policy changes, funding, surveys, or community activities.  
• Request specific actions—complete a form, upload documents, RSVP, pay fees, attend evaluations, or confirm receipt.  
• Occasionally confirm that an earlier action has cleared a hold.

Triage & response  
1. Scan subject line for verbs like “PAST DUE,” “FINAL WARNING,” “REMINDER,” “CONFIRM,” or “URGENT.” Flag these and calendar any deadlines immediately.  
2. For administrative holds, missing docs, or degree matters, reply the same day with required attachments or clarifying questions.  
3. For event/newsletters, skim, register if interested, then archive.  
4. Log funding opportunities separately for follow-up.

Style & policy notes  
• Use Columbia/SEAS salutation conventions (“Dear [Office/Name],”).  
• Keep replies concise, attach PDFs as instructed, and cc advisors only when policy requires.  
• Protect student ID and other sensitive data; never forward without permission.
summaries.Sreedhar Sivakumaran:
Sreedhar Sivakumaran category = Dad’s oversight on family finances & logistics.  

What lands here  
• Messages from Sreedhar—or threads he forwards—about credit-card alerts, duplicate or unexplained charges, rent and utility reminders, tuition notices, medical bills, insurance claims, travel bookings, or vendor invoices.  
• Occasional third-party billing inquiries (e.g., PT clinic, landlord) that require our confirmation.  

Sender intent / common asks  
Sreedhar is flagging a payment, fee, or document that he wants us to verify, pay, dispute, or file. Typical verbs: “did you get…?”, “please check,” “follow up,” “is this yours?”.  

Triage & response  
1. Open statement/portal link, reconcile against our own records.  
2. If charge legitimate, confirm payment date or autopay status.  
3. If error or duplicate, call issuer, start dispute, and update Sreedhar.  
4. Capture key details (amount, date, account) in the shared finance tracker.  
5. Reply within 24 h summarizing action taken; keep tone factual, appreciative, and concise.  

Policies / style  
No PII in replies beyond last 4 digits of account. Copy Mom only when decision or payment is completed.
summaries.WeTransfer:
WeTransfer – Category Summary (120–180 words)

What’s here  
Emails generated by WeTransfer notifying me that photo labs (e.g., CRC Film Process, ElizPhoto, The Film Lab) have sent finished scans or that I—or collaborators—have downloaded a transfer. Subjects follow “<Sender> sent you <filename> via WeTransfer” and always include an expiry date and unique download link.

Typical sender intent / requests  
• Deliver high-resolution image batches of developed film  
• Alert me to expiry so I retrieve them promptly  
• Occasionally ask how to handle remaining physical negatives

Triage & handling  
1. Within 24 h of arrival, verify sender address matches an expected lab.  
2. Download files immediately; save to project-specific folder on NAS → back-up to cloud.  
3. Reply “Files received—thank you” or clarify any lab questions (e.g., keep vs. discard negatives).  
4. Add expiry date to calendar until download confirmed, then archive email to “WeTransfer.”  
5. Delete transfer from local Downloads after backup.

Policies / preferences  
• Never forward WeTransfer links externally.  
• Use lab’s quoted filename as folder name; prepend job ID for traceability.  
• Maintain courteous, concise acknowledgments (<3 sentences).
summaries.ConEdison:
ConEdison  
Scope: Automated service messages from Con Edison (noreply@customerassistance.coned.com, *@billing.coned.com, ConEdison@email.coned.com). Typical content:  
• Monthly bill‐ready notices, due-date reminders, and payment confirmations.  
• On-boarding series (Welcome, Register, Billing) and service-activation confirmations for new addresses or account moves.  

Sender intent & common asks  
Primarily informational: “Your bill is ready,” “It’s time to pay,” “We received your payment,” or “Service will be connected.” Links drive users to pay, view statements, register, or manage accounts. No reply expected.

Handling/triage  
1. Filter into “Utilities › ConEdison.”  
2. Flag “Bill Ready” and “Bill Due” for quick scan of amount + due date; forward to finance tool or set calendar reminder if needed.  
3. Mark “Payment Confirmation” and “Welcome/Service” messages as read and archive after verifying accuracy.  
4. Watch for unusual amounts, address changes, or failed-payment alerts; escalate to billing team within one business day.

Response strategy  
Only reply via Con Edison’s portal or phone per account security policy; never email sensitive account or banking details. Maintain paperless preference and keep latest 12 months of bills saved in cloud storage for audit purposes.
summaries.Internship:
Category Summary — Internship (120–180 words)

This folder houses all career-focused mail—primarily internship, co-op, research-assistant, and early-career job threads. Typical messages include:  

• Application lifecycle updates (submission receipts, status changes, rejections, referrals, next-step forms) from corporate ATS addresses (Amazon, JPMorgan, Tesla, etc.).  
• Recruiter or hiring-manager outreach requesting interviews, documents, or Calendly scheduling.  
• Career-services blasts, event/webinar invites, and feedback surveys from university offices.  
• Faculty or peer notes about research projects or informal internship collaboration.  

Sender intent is either to: 1) acknowledge an application, 2) request an action (complete a form, pick an interview slot, send a résumé/portfolio), or 3) advertise opportunities and resources.

Triage/response:  
1. Scan daily; flag anything requiring time-sensitive actions or deadlines.  
2. Log status in the internship tracker spreadsheet.  
3. Reply within 24–48 h using a concise, professional tone; attach files as PDFs, not links.  
4. Once closed (offer declined, rejection, or completed task), move to “Archive–Internship”.

Policy/constraints: keep correspondence confidential, use school email for university contacts, CC referring parties when asked, and avoid sharing personally identifying details in public channels.
